data.title: Coherent Is The Ultimate AI Bottleneck Play
data.description:
Introduction

Coherent Corp. (COHR) is one of the companies seeing major returns amid 2026 market choppiness. I’m very happy to see the stock do so well, as it was one of my Buying suggestions for 2026 at the end of December ’25, as an AI-supply bottleneck, together with Marvell (MRVL), and Fabrinet (FN). As a matter of fact, all three of them have done exceptionally well this year: COHR is up 88% YTD, MRVL +74% YTD, and FN +53% YTD.

I, for myself, have gone with Marvell, because I thought it was suppressed the most in 2025, but I’m glad to see all three did way better than the market. Amid the fears surrounding large CapEx spending, the market could have dragged the main suppliers of the data-center buildout lower, but gladly, that didn’t happen. Even though investors are worried that this capacity is needless, it doesn’t cancel the fact that the suppliers majorly benefit.

Another reason I thought it would be nice to take a closer look at Coherent is that just yesterday, I broke down POET Technologies (POET), which is possibly the next age of transceivers and data center networking, as it is working towards giving up on electricity for the data-center buildout entirely, using only light. It is still far from achieving this goal, but this is a direct attempt to disrupt companies like Coherent. At the same time, Coherent is a large industry leader, unbothered by the smaller peers, which has some mind-blowing developments on its own, and some large partnerships, which support its leading position.

Also, the company has quarterly earnings in about two weeks from now, on May 5th, after the market, and it looks like Wall Street expects to see a major beat, with 15 positive vs. 1 negative EPS revisions, and 16 positive vs 0 negative revenue revisions. This could be both a blessing and a curse, since usually, when the sentiment before the earnings is this positive, the stock begins gaining weeks before the report. But if the company misses even slightly, this positive sentiment can turn into a major negative catalyst, since the market expectations are through the roof. In this article, I’ll take a look at the latest quarter and try to understand what to expect this time around.

Coherent’s Competitive Position

Quick disclaimer - I don’t have a technical background, but I usually try to understand and explain the technology behind the stock. I believe my understanding is sufficient for investing purposes, but some oversimplification is probable, as I’m not trying to break down the technology itself, just what drives the demand, and as a result, the stock price.

The reason Coherent and its peers are on the rise is that, due to the rapid AI adoption, not only is the compute or memory becoming a bottleneck, but also networking. Before, electricity was moved using copper wires, and it was good enough, since distances between the chips and the demand for bandwidth weren’t large. But now, not only are copper wires insufficient, but also electricity isn’t fast enough to allow modern data centers to work as they were planned to work. For this reason, companies like Coherent are doing so well, since they are developing and producing transceiver modules, which are devices able to convert an electrical signal into a light signal and vice versa, allowing it to be sent via fiber-optic cables, which provide a faster solution and higher bandwidth.

But there are different optical modules, depending on the request of the technology it powers, and this is where the innovation of Coherent comes in. As modern data centers are being built, not only are the newest, most powerful GPUs being used for that, but also, those GPUs are installed into racks, effectively creating small supercomputers, which, when connected, make a very large and very powerful supercomputer.

The difficulty is that, before those “supercomputers” were created, data centers had way less demand for data transmitted per second. Today, for large hyperscalers with enormous cloud data centers, 800G (gigabits per second of data transmission) has become a new standard in the last few years, but soon, even that won’t be enough if many racks are connected. Just to understand how much lower the benchmark was, in the past, up to 100G optical modules were enough for a regular data center, and for a large enterprise, 10G or 25G would be sufficient.

Just a decade ago, 100G was the biggest breakthrough in technology, but today, not only are the companies widely adopting 800G, 1.6T (1600G) is on the way, looking forward to 3.2T and 6.4T.

At the same time, even though everyone is trying so hard to avoid copper usage, and companies that provide those solutions are on a roll, Nvidia (NVDA) CEO Jensen Huang said that:

Nvidia would use copper and co-packaged optics in both its upcoming Vera Rubin Ultra and Feynman lines, with both options advancing in parallel.

After the news, Coherent and some of the optics peers fell, although I don’t view this necessarily as a bad thing. They have a massive partnership, and Nvidia has invested $2 billion in Coherent, which means that the technology is definitely in demand. The second way of looking at this: the fact that optics is being used only partially means that the company still has growth opportunities not only together with the overall GPU and data center market, but also as a substitute to other solutions like copper. That opportunity is still there.

Q2 2026

Talking about the last quarter, the company saw a rapid revenue growth of 22% YoY to $1.69 billion, with an expanding gross margin, and a 35% growth in Earnings Per Share. The Datacenter & Communication segment takes up 72% of the overall revenue, being the main driver of the growth, increasing by an impressive 34%.

The management not only says that this is the main and fastest-growing segment, but they expect the demand there to continue throughout the whole year, powering companies' rapid growth both in Q3 and Q4 of 2026, accelerating even more in fiscal 2027:

In particular, we expect continued strong sequential revenue growth in both our March and June quarters, and we expect our fiscal '27 revenue growth rate to exceed our fiscal '26 growth rate.

To be more specific, 800G and 1.6T transceivers are what drive the demand, together with some additional technologies that allow faster bandwidth, like OCS (Optical Circuit Switching), and CPO solutions (co-packaged optics). This, by the way, is what Jensen Huang referred to when he said they’ll be using it together with copper:

The key growth drivers that we see over the coming quarters are growth in both 800 gig and 1.6T transceivers, growth from the ramps of new products such as OCS and CPO solutions and ongoing exceptionally strong demand in our products for DCI and scale across.

The demand is in fact so strong that the company has a book-to-bill ratio of 4x, meaning they are simply unable to ship as much, as companies are ordering from them. For this reason, the peers of Coherent, like Marvell and Lumentum (LITE), are also seeing the same growth. Even though the industry is competitive, the demand is still bigger than the market-wide supply.

In Q2, we experienced another step function increase in our data center bookings with a book-to-bill ratio that exceeded 4x as customer demand continues to increase and customers place orders further out in time, which provides us with strong visibility for the coming quarters.

As a result, Coherent spends a lot of money to ramp up production with additional manufacturing capacity in the hope of capturing as many orders as possible. The company wants to double its indium phosphide production by Q4, which would allow it to ramp up 6-inch wafer production. A 6-inch wafer allows producing 4 times more chips than a 3-inch wafer, and it will also be twice as cheap.

We expect 1.6T to ramp significantly over the coming quarters with the early phase of the ramp driven by our EML and Silicon Photonics-based transceivers followed by our 200-gig VCSEL-based 1.6T transceivers ramping in the second half of this calendar year. On the supply side to address the extraordinary growth in demand, we are investing in the rapid expansion of our production capacity. For example, we significantly increased our indium phosphide production capacity in Q2, and we are executing on track to our plan to double our internal indium phosphide production capacity by the fourth quarter of this calendar year.

I also found it quite interesting that even with growing demand, the company is working on becoming more effective and productive, boosting operational excellence. They are doing so by closing the site, which didn’t perform well, and they are turning their focus to what is profitable and in demand today. Even though the same as with all of the semiconductor industry, the business of this company is cyclical. As long as the cycle continues, this is a correct strategy, in my opinion:

Last week, we completed the sale of our product division based in Munich, Germany that makes tools for materials processing. The sale of this product division is expected to be immediately accretive to both gross margin and EPS. As a result of this sale and other operational streamlining initiatives, we exited 10 sites over the past quarter which brings the total number of sites that we've either sold or exited to 33 over the past roughly 6 quarters since we began this initiative. We plan to continue to streamline our footprint and exit additional underutilized and unnecessary sites over the coming quarters.

Guidance for the next quarter is optimistic, with $1.77 billion in revenue expected in the middle of the given range, which would mean almost 5% increase QoQ, and 18% growth YoY. Even the lowest range of the revenue guidance would mean 13% revenue growth, which would be a solid increase, since Q3 2025 was an exceptionally strong quarter, showing 24% ToY growth. I remind you, the outlook for the fiscal 2027 is even more optimistic, and the management expects a growth ratio higher than that of 2026.

Gross margin is expected to be between 38.5% to 40.5%, which will be ahead of that a year ago, and if higher than 39%, ahead of the last quarter. Earnings per share are expected to be in line with the Q2 on the lowest end of the range, and to be 15% higher than a quarter before on the high end of the range. On the high end of the range, the growth YoY will be over 62%. The company isn’t guiding for GAAP results, as they can’t predict what the impact of some of the charges will be. Among those are the Munich site, consolidation, currency swings, etc.

By the way, EPS is growing way faster than the revenue and the margins for all of the fixed costs the company has already paid. Basically, the growth they are bringing in right now is kind of cheaper and cheaper, since it comes from R&D and fabs that are ready, and their SG&A or marketing costs are not growing as fast, because the demand is organic.

Valuation

Of course, the main pain is the valuation. On all of the regular metrics, the stock looks incredibly expensive, but that premium looks way healthier on the PEG ratio. PEG ratio is the best way to look at the stocks in the fast-growing industries, since it normalizes the sector for growth, and we can see the real comparison with the peers.

According to the PEG ratio, the stock trades at 1.7 times, ahead of 1.46 for the sector, but it is a premium of about 16%. It is not cheap in any way, but it is also not sky-high as the traditional P/E suggests.

Conclusion

Coherent is a great company in a fast-growing industry. The management makes sure they stay operationally excellent and does everything to capture the fast-growing demand for their products. At the same time, after the rally, COHR stock trades at a high valuation multiple, not only leaving no room for mistakes, but also suggesting that some cooling-off is possible after the rally.

When buying Marvell myself, the stock traded at a premium on the traditional price-to-earnings metrics, but looked cheap on the PEG ratio. This time around, I’m afraid most of the good news has already been priced in, and I would wait for some correction to start a position in COHR stock myself.

data.title: Axcelis Technologies: Medium-Term May Be Priced In, But Overall, A Lot Of Upside
data.description:
Introduction

Axcelis Technologies, Inc. (ACLS) shot up since my last article back in January, and now that we have the full year’s performance released, I want to revisit the past year and see what is still ahead for the company in 2026 and beyond. The share price seems to reflect a positive outlook going forward because the past year does not justify such a price increase in one year.

There’s no need to sell your shares right now, and I suggest holding for a couple of more years at least, since I believe there will be a lot of gains to come.

By the Numbers

Over the last year, the company’s revenues continued their downtrend. In the latest financial year report, revenues came in 17.5% lower than last year. The company continues to be in a cyclical digestion phase across its biggest end markets, with power and general mature customers working through prior capacity additions, while the Chinese demand was digesting earlier investments. It’s not a great look, but this was an expected outcome that was communicated many times throughout the year.

It does seem that there will be some positives in the future, as the graph suggests that there may be a bottom set.

In the latest yearly report, we can also see how the company’s profitability came in, and it is not great either. Gross margins improved slightly to 44.9%, up 20bps, and operating margins took a dive to 14.2% from 20.7%. Operating expenses increased while revenues continued to decline. Unfortunately for ACLS, when system sales softened, the cost structure didn’t move down with volume, so the fixed-cost base weighed on its operating leverage. The only way to improve this is by increasing utilization rates so the fixed costs can be spread out much more efficiently.

The company’s balance sheet remains quite formidable, with over $145m in cash and equivalents, as well as $229m in ST investments against zero debt, apart from operating lease liabilities. This is a great place to be when its operations are soft and are on their way to turning around. The company can focus all of its capital on maintaining its competitiveness and weather the downturn with no issues. It’s hard to go out of business when there is no one knocking on your door and asking for the money that they lent you.

The company’s cash flow did come down slightly from last year, but it is still over $118m, and with capex spending of around $11.3m, its free cash flow was around $107m, which is still pretty good considering the downturn in operations.

Overall, 2025 has not been particularly impressive for Axcelis. The demand for the products has been soft, which squeezed margins considerably compared to last year due to lower system volume in power, general mature, and China, which still accounts for around 32% of total revenues. This type of performance doesn’t warrant its share price going up over 180% in just one year, which means that the analysts and investors are focusing on what is still ahead for the company.

Comments on the Outlook

Upcoming Quarter

For Q1 ’26, analysts are expecting the company to do $0.71 and $0.44 in adjusted and GAAP EPS, respectively, on $195m in revenues. This translates into around 1% growth compared to last year, which may signal that the end of declining revenues is upon us. Management also guided $195m in revenues and non-GAAP EPS of $0.71, but around 38 cents in GAAP diluted EPS. For the full year, the company is expecting to see flat revenue performance, which I see as a positive because it does point to the company approaching the bottom. The growth in memory is expected to offset the continued weakness in power and mature.

Memory Revival

Historically, memory has been a poor segment compared to its power/SiC offerings. We don’t get a precise breakdown of revenues in products, so we don’t know precisely what the memory revenue is as % of total revenues these days, but it is likely around 15%-25%. This is a big enough number that will most likely move the revenue needle considerably now that it is looking to come back to life. Memory players like Micron (MU), Western Digital (WDC), and SanDisk (SNDK) have been one of the best performers in terms of share price gains over the last year, and all because of capacity constraints, which allows them to massively increase the prices of memory products directly, leading to an increase in profitability. We are currently in a super cycle of memory, and anyone exposed to AI and memory like DRAM and HBM has seen their share price performance beat the broader index market by a massive margin. So, of course, Axcelis is finally going to see some traction in its memory business. With Micron being a direct beneficiary of the AI hype as it sells HBM and DRAM into the AI stack, Axcelis benefits when memory manufacturers convert price strength and demand visibility into fab equipment orders. In the past, this conversion lagged the pricing cycle by around 6-18 months, so now, ACLS is seeing some of the conversion flowing through in 2026, and that is only the beginning.

The management said that they expect large tool orders to come more meaningfully when new fabs open at the end of 2026 and into 2027, but the company is already seeing that customers are trying to push through a few more tool orders now, instead of waiting.

I think for this reason, we are seeing a similar situation in the share price performance as we saw in the pure AI memory plays of MU, SNDK, and WDC. Capacity constraints are the key drivers of share price over the last year because investors are already pricing in much higher revenue and profitability that will eventually come sometime in the back end of 2026 and throughout 2027. I am also very bullish on the super cycle lasting much longer than 2027 and well into 2028. The insatiable hunger for power that we see from the hyperscalers right now is not going to end very soon, since we know that the top 5 or 6 hyperscalers this year will spend around $750B in AI capex.

For next year, I am assuming the same spending pattern to come through, which could mean the top hyperscalers will spend $1T in capex on AI. I think the worst case is actually them slowing down, but I don’t think it is going to be a meaningful slowdown. Maybe down to $500B, which is still a ridiculous amount of capital to be tossed around by just a handful of companies.

Veeco Merger

I covered the Veeco (VECO) merger in my previous article, so I won’t go into that right now; however, I will say that when it does happen, the company will see a massive TAM expansion. With Axcelis doing ion implantation while VECO doing laser annealing, the merged company will offer both steps as an integrated solution, which will create yield guarantees that the standalone tools cannot simply match. The two functions are consecutive in nature on the fab floor, so by integrating them, they will offer a meaningful value proposition for chipmakers running high-precision power or AI chip processes where yield losses can cost quite dearly. The merger is viewed quite positively overall and will transform the company tremendously.

Both sides of shareholders approved the deal in early February, and many regulatory clearances were already passed as well. One regulator remains, and that is China’s SAMR. In the latest conference call, the phrasing around China was very deliberate. They said that they are “actively engaging with the authorities in China,” but did not suggest there was an imminent approval on the cards. They still expect the deal to close in 2026, but according to reports, SAMR is taking unusually long to approve the deal. According to Ion Analytics, SAMR usually takes around 18.2 days to review the deal. Still, this time around, it is taking a more cautious approach when handling reviews that involve U.S. companies.

So, it all depends on China. Is it going to pass? That is the hardest question to answer. I don’t think the deal creates a monopoly since the two companies are not in the same area of functions. Also, neither company is the biggest player in the relevant markets, so I don’t think the narrative will be monopoly concerns. A block of a merger by SAMR is a rare occurrence in general, so I am sure it will go through eventually.

Closing Comments

If you already own Axcelis, I don’t see a reason to sell; however, I am not looking to add AI play to my portfolio when I am actively looking to diversify from that specific risk. I am especially not looking to add a company that is trading at a forward P/E ratio of 36x. It seems that the near-term boost has already been priced in, especially after B. Riley Financial upgraded it to a Buy recently and gave a price target of $150.

There is no reason to sell such a winner right now, especially if you are sitting on a decent profit. Let it ride for the next couple of years, until we know what is going to happen to the overall AI demand in late 2027, because for now, I see further growth ahead.

data.title: 2 'Set And Forget' Dividend Legends To Buy Now
data.description:
One of the reasons why I have decided to follow an income-based strategy is that my portfolio results (and progress) are not impacted by what others think.

The success of price-focused strategies is determined by the function of earnings growth and valuation multiple. None of these can be predicted with a robust precision, especially the latter.

There are just so many variables that dictate the path of valuation multiples: e.g., overall sentiment in the market, interest rates, inflation, geopolitics, which President wins and so on.

As a result of this, it may happen that in one year I can exceed my annual return target, the next year have even better success, and then in the third year suddenly fall back to square one. And the worst what could happen is that this "Year 3" coincides with the moment when I have to transition to a withdrawal phase.

Having this kind of unpredictability embedded in my portfolio which is something that will have a significant bearing on a big part of my life is simply unacceptable for me.

I know what the financial theory says (the Miller-Modigliani dividend irrelevance theory): dividends don't create value but rather the underlying cash flows and earrings growth. But as a human being, I have my biases.

I simply must have predictability, stability and all working out as I have planned out to keep peace of mind. And if it means paying some price for that like not capturing alpha-like returns, so be it.

To achieve this, there are two simple things required:

  • Properly calibrated expectation: investment progress and the final goal have to be linked to current income streams (price performance has to be excluded from that equation). For example, the goal could be to build up a portfolio which produces $100,000 of dividends a year and for that to happen until a certain date each year the portfolio has to generate $7,000 higher dividends than in the previous year.
  • Durable investments: the majority of the holdings (or portfolio cash flows) must be associated with investments that can sustain their dividend payments across different market cycles, for multiple decades in the future. Otherwise the predictability (sleep-well-at-night) aspect is not there, just as for price-based strategies.

Now, with this in mind, let me share two durable dividend producing picks for "zero nighttime anxiety" and maximized investment result (progress) predictability.

#1: MAIN, 7.9%

Main Street Capital (MAIN) is a BDC, which provides private credit financing to lower-middle-market (or LMM) companies. The optics already here do not look good because of the word "private credit".

However, even though I am a structural BDC bull and prefer several other BDCs over MAIN, when it comes to maximized income stability and stress-free cash flows, I view MAIN as the best BDC pick which nicely fulfills these two aspects.

MAIN has one of the longest track-records in the BDC sector (BIZD); almost 20 years as a publicly traded company. And this is how it has performed since its IPO:

It is an income investor's dream scenario: very gradual price appreciation with juicy dividends which overtime compound to something big and beautiful. The only thing that is required is a) being patient and b) reinvesting the collected dividends to accelerate compounding.

Now, the question is whether future will be materially different from the past.

There are two components to this answer:

1) Price direction: as an income-investors I really don't care so much about where the price will go from here as long as the underlying fundamentals and dividends remain stable.

2) Dividend stability and growth: this is what is critically important for me. The good thing is that if we look at the fundamentals, we will see all the major data points signaling that MAIN should be able to deliver consistent dividends going forward.

For the dividends to stay safe, there are a couple of pre-conditions that must be met. I will underscore the three main ones.

First, MAIN has the fourth least leveraged balance sheet. The sector average debt-to-equity is 1.25x, but for MAIN the metric stands at 0.81x. This kind of level helps keep the consequences from potential hiccups in the portfolio muted as there is no meaningful leverage that could magnify the downside. Also, MAIN's balance sheet is rated at investment grade, which confirms the inherent resiliency.

Second, MAIN has a top-quality portfolio. It is not just the below sector average non-accrual statistic which validates that, but also the financial situation at the portfolio company level. For instance, the average interest coverage ratio for MAIN's portfolio companies is 4.1x, which is the second strongest metric in the entire BDC space (and I'm talking about both private and public BDCs here).

Third, MAIN has several levers to pull in order to drive earning growth, which is, obviously, positive for the dividend (the best defense is a good offense).

The leverage aspect that I mentioned above is an important one as it shows how much wiggle room MAIN has in using debt to expand portfolio while not assuming elevated financial risks. Another aspect, which comes in handy in the context of earnings growth (and dividend sustainability) is the sector-leading base dividend coverage, which stands at 142%. From the outearned base dividend MAIN can distribute enticing special dividends (which I'm not factoring into my long-term portfolio cash flow projections as they are inherently volatile) or use this capital to grow the portfolio.

Finally, the fact that MAIN trades at the highest P/NAV in the sector is a blessing and a curse.

The curse is that it, theoretically, creates a more difficult environment for MAIN to register strong price appreciation that would be, say, driven by multiple expansion. Again, as a pure income investor I don't care about this aspect.

The blessing is that MAIN can source fresh equity via accretive share issuances. It is just yet another source of capital for MAIN to grow the portfolio and drive NII per share growth.

Plus, in the past 12 months, only two BDCs increased their base dividends (by 4.1% on a year-over-year basis). MAIN was one of them, which serves as the icing on the cake and confirms its 'sleep-well-at-night' appeal.

#2: EPD, 6.0%

Enterprise Products Partners L.P. (EPD) is one of the biggest midstream partnerships or MLPs out there with a market cap of over $79 billion.

Just as MAIN, EPD has perfectly clean dividend track-record, meaning that there have not been any base distributions cuts since the IPO. In fact, EPD is a dividend aristocrat with 28 consecutive years of distribution increases.

If we look at the performance chart of EPD below and compare it to that of MAIN above, we would notice a great similarity:

A steady price dynamic with strongly compounding current income component.

Now, just as for MAIN, the question is whether EPD has what it takes to "walk my talk" (i.e., to deliver durable cash flows without recording NAV or value impairments).

In my humble opinion, EPD is more than well-positioned to deliver on that.

I will give you 3 reasons.

First, EPD has, arguably, the best balance sheet in the entire midstream (MLP and c-corp) space. It has an upper investment grade balance sheet, which is quite rare. Most midstream businesses either don't have IG ratings at all or have them at medium IG level. Another aspect, which makes the balance sheet (or rather financial risk angle) even more compelling is the fact that 98% of borrowings are assumed at fixed rate. The weighted average cost of debt is 4.7%, which is close to the current market level financing rates.

Second, and this is just as important as the first point, the EPD's underlying business model is inherently de-risked thanks to the presence of very favorable contracts. Namely, the lion's share of EPD's EBITDA generation stems from long-term "take-or-pay" contracts, which are stipulated with large and bankable corporates (i.e., infrastructure users). These contracts come with minimal volume and price clauses, which mitigate both volumetric and commodity price risks. On top of that, most of these contracts are linked to annual fee escalators or a CPI factor, which add a nice organic growth flavor to the already enticing situation. In essence, these points renders EPD similar to a fixed income play (especially if we couple it with the first point above).

Third, EPD carries a huge margin of safety for its distributions. The current distributions are more than covered by the distributable cash flow generation; the ratio is 1.7x. As in MAIN's case, EPD has the luxury to use these surplus (retained) cash flows to fund growth. Special distributions have not been EPD's thing (not a single special since 1998). Given that the balance sheet is already at an upper IG level, debt reduction activity is very unlikely. What EPD has communicated is that it will consider utilizing the confirmed buyback capacity, while ensuring steady distribution growth and, importantly, a healthy organic CapEx program. Namely, EPD is in a position to not only strongly preserve the current distributions, but also grow them on a go forward basis.

Finally, what makes me extra comfortable with EPD is that 33% of common units are owned by the management and affiliates. It just shows how strong an alignment there is between the insiders and us, long-term investors. It is the same thing for MAIN as it is an internally managed BDC (as opposed to "90%+" of other peers which are externally managed).

data.title: Advance Auto Parts: Balance Sheet Stress And Market Share Losses
data.description:
Advance Auto Parts (AAP) is trying to reinvent itself. Poor leadership and share losses to peers have caused a near 75% drop in the stock over the last five years, so now the company is contending with getting back on solid ground. While the company has certainly taken steps to clean up its portfolio and improve operating margins from a very low base, I believe this is a considerably risky asset that will only further donate share to peers and believe that the stock is substantially overvalued, to the tune of about 50%.

A Windy Road Full of Debt

For context, Advance operates in one of the best consumer retail divisions - auto aftermarket. The business is not particularly complex in that they sell everything from motor oil to wipers to spark plugs and filters, but also provide "DIFM" services - if your engine breaks down, they can fix it for you. The industry is rooted in cost plus, meaning inflation in parts and labor over time flows through inventory and passed on to the consumer. Auto aftermarket companies tend to not absorb pricing and the industry has remained rational on pricing for decades. The industry is highly consolidated with the top 3 players having over 50% market share.

Over the past two years, the company was succumbing to its debt burden and chose to make strategic moves like selling Worldpac for $1.5 billion to Carlyle. This has been helpful in reducing balance sheet stress and getting the company back in a position where it can focus more on operations.

This is a business that can compound at a low to mid single digit growth rate each year, put forth a modest amount of operating margin expansion each year and deliver high free cash flow conversion such that there is a compelling total return profile for prospective investors. As a result, the industry is highly defensive.

Financial Profile

Let's start with guidance to unpack where this is going:

  • Net sales $8.485bn-$8.575bn
  • SSS% 1-2%
  • Adj. operating margin 3.8%-4.5%
  • Adj. EPS $2.40-$3.10
  • Capex $300 million
  • FCF $100 million
  • Store openings 40-45, market hub openings 10-15

The biggest opportunity for AAP and its stock to see strength is to get the adjusted operating income margin up and to start to close the gap with peers. At just 2.5% in 2025, they pale in comparison to larger peers AutoZone (AZO) and O'Reilly (ORLY).

AZO has an operating margin profile of 19% while ORLY sports a similar profile of mid-19%. AAP is the publicly-listed weaker player and has been suffering form market share losses versus these two for years. The best barometer of growth for this industry is domestic same store sales growth, for which ORLY has compounded in the mid-single digit range over the last five years as has AZO. Now, AZO has slowed down quite a bit towards the low-single digit range, but it was still able to put up over 3% domestic same store sales growth in 2025. Compare this to AAP who has produced near zero growth the last four years and its last year of growth was in the immediate post-COVID aftermath when the sector was growing well above trend.

The other issue with AAP is that they are behind on store labor and in-box investment. One of the biggest issues in retail the last few years has been rising labor costs - wages have gone up and even without mandated minimums being $15/hour, many retailers have had to go there otherwise they risk high talent turnover (which begets even more training costs).

AAP operates a network of over 4,305 stores. It typically takes 1-2 employees to operate a box if a company is being very lean about their operations, not including a DIFM specialist/mechanic for stores that offer complex non-DIY repairs. To effectively run an auto aftermarket box, the right number is about 5-15 employees with a mix of part-time and full-time individuals.

To try and calculate labor costs, we know at year end that employee count was approximately 54,000. If you assume that the average employee works 40 hours per week, that works out to about 112 million labor hours per year at a cost of about $1.8 billion. Total SG&A per year is approximately $3.5 billion so you can see that unless they're operating at the absolute bare minimum, this is onerous. Labor costs are over half of the company's operating expense line. That's a large fixed burden that takes considerable sales growth to lever year after year. The issue is not that they have to pay people, but they have to keep pace with an industry where talent at Walmart (WMT), Costco (COST), and Target (TGT) can be over $18/hour, if not in excess of $20/hour in certain markets. ORLY and AZO have been deleveraging on SG&A in the past two years because of increased labor costs and the persistent need to have made investments. AAP has not done that.

A Highly Leveraged Retailer Carries Considerable Risk

Defining leverage is different for every company depending on the operating structure. AAP doesn't own its real estate any more and is in more of an asset-lite structure, serving more as an operating company of the store base.

For starters, the company uses EBITDAR in their base calculation and with rent expense being over half of the balance of EBITDAR, it deflates the true leverage ratio dramatically. That's a big issue. I believe this is being done to compensate for the lack of an owned asset base which would otherwise put forth a higher depreciation figure that could be used in a simple EBITDA calculation.

Take their LTM leverage update from last quarter. Rent expense at $557 million is over half of the $1.05 billion in adjusted EBITDAR.

Source: Investor Presentation

They supposedly have $3 billion in cash on the balance sheet, but that's not totally accurate.

In 2025, the company issued senior notes of $1.5 billion across two tranches - 2030 and 2033. The proceeds they got from the senior notes offering were put towards the payment of the 5.9% March '26 senior notes and general corporate purposes (split $300 million and $1.2 billion). Effectively, the cash hasn't been spent from a reporting standpoint yet, so it gets added to the balance sheet.

I should note here that they're not likely to retire the '27's any time soon; earliest they would consider doing that is calendar Q4 of this year.

Source: AAP 10-K

The proof is in the details, however. See, the bulk of the cash is going towards being an asset that their ABL facility needs. ABL facilities are secured debt - backed by assets. AAP essentially has one of the muddiest asset structures in all of consumer discretionary, so they quite literally had to raise debt in order to put up assets.

Thus, that's not true "cash" assets they get to keep and delever the balance sheet or put towards growth initiatives. That's cash getting converted as collateral to have an ABL facility. Now look, they can have that cash at some point, but they would lose their ABL. For a business that has large working capital requirements, that's essentially a non-starter. Vendors will accelerate receivables at that point.

So, here's what the actual debt structure is:

  • Senior Notes (Unsecured) $3.45 billion
  • 0 ABL drawn, $896 million available to draw upon
  • Cash = $3.1 billion less qualified cash of $2.3 billion (collateral) = $800 million

Actual AAP Net Debt: $2.65 billion...and this is before we try and do an operating lease/EBITDAR calculation.

Actual AAP adjusted EBITDA should be viewed on a forward basis not an LTM basis (credit rating agencies care about LTM, but a true leverage profile is best viewed on a forward basis). Based upon their guidance of adjusted operating income margin of 4.15% at the midpoint against $8.53bn in sales at the midpoint, we arrive at adjusted operating income of $353 million. D&A last year was 3.1% of sales so rolling that forward to FY26 we get $269 million in D&A. That creates adj. EBITDA of $623 million.

That means that net leverage/adj. EBITDA is approximately 4.24x. That's considerably higher than their posted 2.4x in the last 8-K.

To try and reconcile with the company, I'll also present the lease liability version of leverage. AAP has a significant store footprint in the US and as such it's subject to rent payments. These rent payments are normal operating costs of doing business but the rent owed is a liability that the company has to contend with if there is ever an acceleration of debt or bankruptcy.

Rent last year was approximately 6.4% of sales. This is helpful as many companies won't actually disclose their rent expense. Operating lease liabilities on the balance sheet were $1.81 billion at year end. This is just long-term operating lease liabilities. The current portion is $435 million. Working capital is needed to pay that (hence, you can start to see the reason why that ABL facility is so critical and that the qualified cash as collateral is not actual cash the company can work with). So, total rent liability is about $2.25 billion. The calculation for net debt + operating lease liability leverage is rather simple. Net debt we've established is $2.65 billion. We add in the operating lease liability to get to $4.9 billion. Adj. EBITDAR then becomes rent plus adjusted EBITDA to get to $1.175 billion. Leverage then is 4.2x, similar to our net debt/EBITDA leverage calculation. This makes sense as the two calculations should be relatively similar for a company of this type.

What's AAP Worth?

This is a stock that has lost 71% of its value in the last 5 years. Despite these losses, the valuation quite simply doesn't make sense to look at on a traditional comparable basis. The stock is trading at a considerably elevated P/E multiple and it should not be because of its heightened leverage profile and market share losses to peers.

ORLY trades at 29x my forward EPS of $3.25 (guidance $3.10-$3.20) while AZO trades at 23.2x my forward EPS of $153 (no guidance, AZO does not guide). AAP, if you assumed they could hit the midpoint of their guidance this year, then currently trades at 20.9x. Both ORLY and AZO are not particularly levered entities. The highest is AZO who on net debt/EBITDA LTM basis is 2.0x levered and about 1.8x on a forward basis.

Given a very high leverage ratio for AAP with substantial operating risk and persistent market share losses, this should be trading in the low-double digit P/E range. This would imply that fair value for shares is around $30 per share versus their current level of $57. That represents approximately 47% downside from the current level.

Risks

AAP is a highly leveraged entity with a short base that is considerably large with 9 days to cover/17% of the float. In the event of a risk-on, high beta rally, the stock could squeeze shorts. Additionally, another risk is that operating margins improve organically if the company can start to see real top line growth, but this has not happened in a material way in years. Some may contend that M&A is a risk, but neither AZO nor ORLY would be interested to take on such a heavily levered vehicle and private equity not a likely buyer for the same reason, especially as the company is behind on both in-store POS and labor investments relative to peers.

Conclusion

AAP is a significantly leveraged entity and while the stock trades at a comfortable multiple of nearly 21x earnings, it is heavily laden with debt and there is no clear strategy to stemming the market share losses. Fair value seems to be considerably lowered and I believe the lack of direct information about the true state of its financial profile is what is keeping the stock presently elevated.

data.title: Cameco: A Buy For The Nuclear Future When It Dips Under $120
data.description:
Last week, I covered one of the standout individual stocks in the VanEck Semiconductor ETF (SMH) I’ve been writing about for a while. So, I thought this week I would cover the standout stock in a pair of ETFs I’ve been following for a long time as well.

If you’ve read my coverage of both the VanEck Uranium and Nuclear ETF (NLR) and the Global X Uranium ETF (URA), you know I’m extremely bullish on nuclear energy. It has been entering a bit of a renaissance in the last few years, and the frontier of nuclear engineering is being explored with small modular reactor and liquid-metal cooling technology.

But one thing that both the current generation of nuclear reactors and the advanced prototypes need is uranium. And there’s one company in that space that stands head and shoulders above the rest.

It’s the number one holding in both NLR and URA and it’s the top performer in my personal portfolio (up 57% since I bought in August), it’s called Cameco (CCJ) and it’s a stock you need to consider if you agree with me on nuclear power.

But first, because it’s central to the bull case for Cameco, allow me reiterate why I’m so bullish on the atomic future…

The Opportunity: To Steal Fire from the Gods

The most famous biography of J. Robert Oppenheimer, the father of the atomic bomb, is entitled American Prometheus. It’s a reference to Prometheus, the Titan of Greek mythology, who defied Zeus and stole fire from the gods of Olympus to give to humanity.

While the Oppenheimer biography is obviously alluding to the destructive fire of the atomic bomb, the Prometheus of myth gave mankind the gifts of technology, knowledge, and civilization with his gift of fire.

We used nuclear technology for weapons at first, but atomic fission may just be our salvation from ecological and energy crises when used peacefully.

Nuclear energy is absolutely wild when you stop to think about it. We literally split apart atoms, the fundamental building blocks of reality, and we use the heat to… boil water. In that way it’s not particularly fancy, most electricity is generated by boiling water.

But you don’t need to burn anything to generate power with uranium. And that’s why nuclear is the ultimate clean energy proposition. What you might interpret as smoke coming out of the massive cooling towers at many nuclear reactors is nothing more than steam from the boiling water.

Nuclear is extremely efficient and it can generate power rain or shine unlike its fellow clean energy technologies like wind and solar. Now, please take a look at my favorite chart to illustrate my point here.

Nuclear energy often gets a bad reputation because of two disasters in its history. But even if you include all the deaths caused by the Chernobyl and Fukushima disasters together, nuclear is the second safest power source behind only solar, and not by much.

The air pollution from natural gas alone causes 93 times more death than nuclear accidents have. And that’s the “cleanest” option we have among the fossil fuels.

Nuclear also generates only a little more than half the total greenhouse gas emissions as wind power does. Wind turbine blades wear out after about 25 years under ideal circumstances and then they need to be either buried or burned.

Meanwhile, your average nuclear reactor can continue operating for about 40 years and with modern life extension technology they can be kept running for up to 80 years. And, according to the Department of Energy, they potentially might be able to last even longer.

The best part is, none of this is theoretical. There’s already a country that has switched the bulk of its grid from fossil fuel to nuclear power – France.

The French Connection

France began switching from fossil fuels to nuclear in the early 1970s as a response to the Energy Crisis of that decade, which began with the 1973 Oil Embargo. The Prime Minister of France at the time, Pierre Messmer put forward the Messmer Plan which accelerated France’s nuclear program and began the construction of 13 new nuclear reactors.

It took about 20 years to complete, but by the time the 1990s rolled around it was clear the Messmer Plan was a total success. Today, France generates about 67% of its power from nuclear energy with most of the remainder coming from hydropower and wind. Fossil fuels only produce a small portion of the country’s electricity.

The result is the fact that France has some of the cleanest air in Europe and its economy is very insulated from oil crises in the Middle East. Given recent events, Pierre Messmer might have been the single most forward-thinking politician of the last century when it comes to energy policy.

Prometheus' Gift

Speaking of recent events in the Middle East, I think that situation will only accelerate the plans many countries have put forward to adopt more nuclear power generation.

It’s a trend that was already happening. According to the World Nuclear Association, there are 75 new nuclear reactors under construction around the world with another 120 planned.

The United States Department of Energy has set a goal to triple America’s nuclear production by the middle of the century. The country is already the largest producer of nuclear power by raw output but it only makes up just shy of 20% of the country’s total power.

South Korea, one of the countries most dependent on energy exports from the Persian Gulf, already generates about one-third of its power from its nuclear fleet. Earlier this year it announced plans for two new massive nuclear reactors to be completed by 2038.

In Japan, the place where the Fukushima disaster occurred, has been rebooting its nuclear plants and returning to its large use of atomic power. Since 2015 it has restarted about half of its 33 nuclear plants and the country’s Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi seems to want to reactivate even more of them.

China alone has 58 reactors in operation that generate 5% of the country’s power with another 33 under construction. Give it a few years and China may rival the United States’ nuclear production.

All those reactors, both new and reactivated, will need uranium. Demand for nuclear power around the world is skyrocketing. It’s the catalyst that will drive Cameco forward for years to come as one of the world’s leading producers of the spicy yellow rock this technology is built upon.

The Money: Full-Spectrum Fuel Play

Based in Saskatoon, Saskatchewan, Cameco is Canada’s premier uranium miner and the second largest in the world in terms of production. In 2025 it was responsible for 15% of the 164 million pounds of uranium produced globally that year.

It was behind only Kazakhstan’s state-run company Kazatomprom and ahead of Russia’s Uranium One which is a subsidiary of Rosatom, the country’s nuclear energy company. And, despite its lower production total than Kazatomprom, Cameco has one very strong competitive edge its Kazakh rival lacks.

Cameco’s main uranium mines, McArthur River/Key Lake and Cigar Lake are both incredibly high-grade mines. The ore they produce has significantly more uranium in it than most.

McArthur River is the world’s largest high-grade uranium mine. It has an average greade of 6.48% and enough reserves to continue producing until 2044 with an average cost of CA$20.31 per pound of uranium extracted which is about $15 American.

Cigar Lake is a much smaller mine but it has an average grade of 16.33%. It has enough reserves to continue producing until 2036 and it costs Cameco CA$21.12 to extract a pound of uranium from it, also about $15 American.

The final mine is the company’s joint venture in Inkai, Kazakstan which only has an average grade of 0.03% but it only costs Cameco CA$12.62 (about $9 American) to extract a pound of uranium from it. It has enough uranium to produce until 2045.

With uranium currently going for $85 per pound that allows Cameco to generate some serious profit from each pound of uranium extracted. It’s also worth noting that prior to the war between America, Iran, and Israel, uranium was the only energy resource that had gone up in value over the previous 12 months. As it stands, uranium is up 32.8% over the past year.

Cameco’s two most productive mines are McArthur River and Cigar Lake which produced 15.1 million pounds and 19.1 million pounds of uranium respectively in 2025. The Inkai mine produced 8.4 million pounds.

The competitive edge Cameco has is that Kazakhstan’s uranium reserves, despite being incredibly large, have an average grade of less than 1%. It takes a lot more ore to get useable uranium from a Kazakh mine than it does from the high-grade ore produced at Cameco’s Canadian mines.

And Cameco’s advantages don’t stop with its fantastic assets.

The company is also engaged in uranium fuel refining and production through its Blind River Refinery, Port Hope Conversion Facility, and Cameco Fuel Manufacturing subsidiary. In 2025, Cameco produced 14 million kilograms of finished uranium fuel.

And that fuel might very well be going into some reactors Cameco also profits from.

In November 2023, Cameco and Brookfield Asset Management set up joint venture ownership of Westinghouse. Cameco owns 49% of the company with Brookfield holding 51%.

Westinghouse produces the AP1000 reactor, which is the most advanced commercially-available nuclear reactor on the market today. It’s in high demand globally. The U.S. has 2 AP1000 reactors in operation with 10 planned. China has 4 with 14 under construction. India has selected for 6 of them, Poland has contracted for 3, Ukraine for 9, and Czechia and Bulgaria for 2 each.

Interestingly enough, once those reactors in India are completed, it’s almost certain Cameco uranium will be powering them. In early March, Cameco and India’s Department of Atomic Energy signed a $1.9 billion agreement that will see 22 million pounds of uranium ore concentrate supplied to India from 2027 to 2035.

Even better, Cameco really has no peers in terms of uranium miners you can invest in. Most of them are state-run like Kazatomprom and Uranium One/Rosatom but of the handful that are publicly traded companies, Cameco dwarfs them all.

Cameco produced 21 million pounds of uranium in 2025. Australia’s Boss Energy (BQSSF) produced 871,000 pounds last year.

The United States’ Uranium Energy Corporation (UEC) produced 130,000 pounds. Another American company, Energy Fuels Inc. (UUUU) mined about 1.7 million pounds of uranium and just over 1 million pounds processed in 2025.

And Australia’s Paladin Energy (PALAF) produced 3 million pounds last year. None of them come anywhere near Cameco, which produced 7 times as much uranium as Paladin in 2025.

So, by traditional valuation metrics, Cameco is difficult to figure out. With so few peers there isn’t much to make an apples to apples comparison with. But the fact that its TTM P/E ratio is 122 forward P/E is 99 is likely not great. However, that’s mitigated significantly by the company’s incredible growth.

Cameco’s TTM PEG ratio is 0.5 and its forward PEG is 1.2 which indicates it may only be slightly overvalued relative to its growth figures. That tracks when you consider the company saw its EPS leap 114% from $0.67 for 2024 to $1.44 for 2025 and that’s on the back of just 11% revenue growth over the same time period.

Also of note is the company’s healthy net profit margin of 16.9% which is impressive when compared to the likes of UUUU at -130% and UEC at -403%. It’s expensive to build a uranium mine so the fact that Cameco is turning a healthy profit is very nice to see. The company also has a very healthy balance sheet with a debt-to-equity ratio of 0.14.

Low debt is something I highly value in any company but, for an industry as capital intensive as mining, combining low debt with solid profit margins is an enormous green flag.

In terms of momentum, Cameco has stalled out a bit in recent months but it’s still up 193% over the past year and I think in the long-term it’s going to go even higher.

Its RSI is sitting just below 62 which is higher than I like to see. However, it tells me that despite nearly tripling in share price over the past 12 months, Cameco still has plenty of runway left before it’s at risk of a bearish reversal.

The last time Cameco exceeded an RSI of 70 and experienced a reversal was back in January, Its share price hit $134 and its RSI peaked at 84. But that correction bottomed out in late March around $100 and Cameco is going back up, it’s sitting just above $120 as I write this.

What that suggests to me is that there is significant buying pressure keeping Cameco up. Investors see the value in the long-term, so they buy the dip to keep their dollar cost average low and send the stock back up. That’s more or less what I’m suggesting you do.

Wall Street analysts have an average 1-year price target of $130 for Cameco with a maximum of $162 and a minimum of $109.

If we take Cameco’s year-end 2026 EPS estimate of $1.20 and multiply it by the company’s year-end P/E projection of 105.87 we get a price target of $127 for the end of the year. Based on Wall Street’s expectations I think that’s probably fair. The nuclear energy trend will take years to play out so for now I would say buy Cameco if it dips into the $115-$120 range and definitely if it gets down close to $100 again. But do not buy over $120, at least until the end of the year.

If Cameco is able to maintain its current P/E ratio of 122 (122 times $1.20) then prices might get as high as $146. In which case, you’ll be very pleased if you can get in at close to $100 when it dips next. That said, I don’t think sub-$100 prices are coming back anytime soon barring a stock split.

In the much longer-term I trust EPS estimates far less, but let’s take the current 2029 EPS estimate of $2.73 and multiply it by both the company’s current P/E of 122 and its forward P/E projection for 2029 which is 43.20.

If Cameco stays near its current valuation that’s a price target of over $300 come the end of 2029 and if its P/E is sitting around 43.20 then that gives us a target of about $120. Split the difference and we arrive at a midpoint of $210 by the end of the decade, which is a roughly 75% increase over current prices, and I think is entirely possible.

Still, for the remainder of 2026 Cameco is a buy from me if it dips into the $110-$115 range and especially if it goes below $110. But don’t buy it if it’s over $120. Keep an eye on Cameco’s RSI and if it gets above 70, watch for a reversal.

Uranium has been on a bullish trend (admittedly with a couple of major reversals) for the past decade and has even exceeded $100 per pound once in that time frame. It’s ticking back up after another reversal earlier this year.

If uranium prices stay elevated (which I think they will due to increased demand from new and reactivated reactors) Cameco is an incredible long-term buy because it’s already profitable at current prices and isn’t racing to achieve profitability like many of its smaller peers. It has only two real rivals globally and there are few western countries that will be eager to buy uranium from either Russia or Kazakhstan if they have another option.

Cameco, and the Canadian uranium that makes up the bulk of its production, is that other option. And it has the largest producer of nuclear power in the world right next door. President Trump, even with all of his tariffs on Canada, realizes Canadian energy imports are vital and there is a carveout in the president’s tariffs on Canada for uranium which is tariffed at just 10%.

Aside from America, Cameco is selling vast amounts of uranium to India and many other countries and its state of the art AP1000 reactors are in high demand globally. If you want to target a single stock from either of the nuclear ETFs I’ve covered in the past, Cameco is easily the strongest prospect.

But there are risks. The biggest risk to nuclear energy is politics, and the biggest risk to Cameco as a stock is its valuation.

The Risks: Avoiding a Valuation Meltdown

First, the risk to nuclear power.

The primary risk to nuclear energy isn’t the possibility of meltdowns or a lack of uranium. There have really only been five major nuclear disasters in history, including Chernobyl and Fukushima. And if you refer back to the chart earlier, even if you include all the deaths from those disasters, nuclear is still the second safest form of power generation.

However, following the nuclear disaster in Fukishima, Germany abandoned nuclear power. The country shut down its last reactor in 2023 and has no plans to reactivate any of them, even after Russia caused chaos in Germany and Europe more broadly by throttling the supply of natural gas it exports to the continent.

So, even with all the promise nuclear power presents as a source of vast amounts of low-emission energy, politicians can still always get in the way. The current administration in the U.S. is very pro-nuclear, so are many governments worldwide, but the success of nuclear energy is dependent on the political will for it remaining high.

As a stock, Cameco’s main risk is its potential overvaluation. Even if there isn’t much to compare it to in terms of scale, trading at 100 times earnings is not likely to be sustainable. Based on its current P/E projections and earnings projections, the market seems to have decided $120 is about fair value for the stock. See the PEG ratio I mentioned above.

So, if it dips below $120 consider buying but I wouldn’t buy at over $120 right now. In the long-term I think this stock is strong, and it has very solid fundamentals. But its P/E ratio is concerning. Once that cools off I will reassess the stock but it will remain a risk as long as it stays this high.

The Verdict: Buy the Future When it Dips, Beware Overvaluation

I think, based on everything I’ve presented to you here, that I’m right in thinking that nuclear energy is the future. We’ve been able to generate power from splitting atoms since the 1950s but nuclear technology is finally getting the attention it deserves.

And no matter which direction nuclear power moves in from here, be it small modular reactors, liquid metal-cooling, whatever, uranium will likely remain the key resource needed to fuel the reactors powering the future, no matter what shape they come in.

Cameco is the best single-stock play on nuclear given its strong fundamentals and presence in almost every stage of the nuclear fuel cycle. It’s a buy for me whenever it dips below $120, but especially if it drops below $115. The closer you can get to $100 the better.

Because, in the long-term, I am incredibly bullish on the funny yellow rocks we can use to boil water without burning anything.

data.title: Buy The Dip: Up To 11% Yields Looking Way Too Cheap
data.description:
My investment strategy is opportunistic capital recycling centric, which means that I identify quality businesses with solid balance sheets and stable cash flow profiles, and then wait for them to fall out of favor with Mr. Market. Once they do, I buy the dip, wait for them to move back into favor with Mr. Market while collecting a hefty dividend, and then rinse and repeat.

With this in view, in this article, I'm going to share two stocks that yield up to 11.7% at the moment, that are traded at huge discounts to their net asset value, have solid balance sheets, and offer very attractive risk-adjusted total returns.

A Deeply Discounted Timberland REIT With Hidden Upside

The first up I want to discuss is Rayonier (RYN), which is a land resources REIT (VNQ) that focuses primarily on timber. It owns about 4.1 million acres of timberland across the U.S., with the vast majority of it in the U.S. South and Southeast and over 900,000 acres in the Pacific Northwest. It also owns six sawmills that produce 1.2 billion board feet of lumber annually and has an active real estate development platform that is currently running three master-planned communities in the Southern United States. RYN also recently completed its merger with PotlatchDeltic, which expects to deliver $40 million in run-rate synergies, adding material value to the business.

I really like Timberland as an investment because it is a proven inflation hedge. The underlying land itself is also very finite, located in attractive geographies, and has numerous higher and better alternative uses, including:

  • Solar leases (it already has solar options covering 80,000 acres)

  • Carbon capture leases that cover 154,000 acres

  • Opportunities to convert it into single-family developments

All of these alternatives can increase the value per acre by between 5 and 15 times, making them high-upside options for management to unlock significant value for shareholders.

Why The Discount to NAV Creates a Compelling Entry Point

Meanwhile, RYN trades at a steep 35% discount to its analyst consensus NAV when simply looking at the timberland on its own merits, and not even considering these higher and better use alternatives. In addition to the high valuation multiple expansion potential from simply trading in line with its private market value, as well as the higher and better use option, it pays investors with a 5.1% dividend yield. It also has a strong balance sheet with an investment-grade credit rating from both S&P and Moody's, and its net debt to adjusted EBITDA is well below its target leverage level. Therefore, it has plenty of capacity to buy back stock aggressively, which should also significantly boost NAV per share, given how deep the discount to NAV currently is in the stock price.

That said, RYN is not risk-free, as timber markets currently are depressed due to a weak housing market, and the company will need to successfully complete its integration with PotlatchDeltic and unlock the synergies it expects to. It will also need to execute well on stock repurchases while managing its balance sheet as well as its higher and better use strategy in order to truly unlock the deeply embedded value in its shares. Overall, though, I think the long-term risk-reward profile is exceptionally attractive in RYN stock right now.

A Defensively Structured BDC Hiding in Plain Sight

The other opportunity to discuss is Morgan Stanley Direct Lending (MSDL), which is a business development company (BIZD) managed by Morgan Stanley (MS), which is a highly reputable asset manager with nearly $2 trillion in assets under management and a 40-year track record in alternatives, including significant experience and capabilities in direct lending.

MSDL is very defensively structured, with a focus on non-cyclical industries and 98% of the portfolio invested in senior secured debt, including 96% of the portfolio in first-lien senior secured debt, diversified across 35 industries and 227 borrowers, which means that its average position size is simply 40 basis points. It also has one of the lower fee structures among externally managed BDCs, while MS is well aligned with MSDL shareholders since it owns 11.3% of MSDL's equity.

Portfolio Quality That Stands Out Among BDC Peers

Its portfolio is performing quite well, with one of the lowest levels of PIK (payment-in-kind) income in the entire sector at just 3.9%. Its non-accruals are just 1.6% on a cost basis, which is also well below many of its peers. With just 3.3% of its portfolio at risk rating 3 and only 1% at risk rating 4, it has a very small watch list, especially relative to many of its peers. With a 40% weighted average loan-to-value, the portfolio is also quite defensively positioned and poised to get solid recoveries even in the event that some of its investments do go into default.

The company recently reset its dividend by reducing it by 10% to 45 cents per share due to Federal Reserve rate cuts and spread compression. However, even after this reduction, it still yields 11.7%, and it covers its dividend comfortably, as it generated 49 cents per share in NII (net investment income) this past quarter.

Why The Discount and Buybacks Could Be Transformative

Its balance sheet is also quite flexible, with net debt to equity of about 1.14 times, which is well below its 1.25 times upper limit. This gives it room to further ramp its NII simply by adding leverage to the balance sheet, ideally through stock buybacks, which management has emphasized as a major priority. In fact, it has a $100 million buyback program that is active, and it is deploying capital fairly aggressively into it. Given that it's a $1.3 billion market cap company, this could be highly accretive to NAV and NII per share.

Additionally, it recently launched a joint venture that is at about 50% of its target size and expects to continue ramping in order to grow its NII per share to further support its dividend moving forward. With a 24% discount to NAV, it has one of the highest NAV discounts in the entire sector, which is well above that of Blackstone Secured Lending (BXSL) and Ares Capital Corporation (ARCC) and is roughly on par with Blue Owl Capital Corporation's (OBDC) discount to NAV.

The Risk Picture, And Why I'm Still Highly Convicted on Both

While MSDL does have 20% exposure to software, it has been employing numerous risk mitigation techniques, including a proprietary AI scorecard that is applied to every investment and updated quarterly. It is also focusing on mission-critical system-of-record platforms that should be among the most resistant to AI disruption. Between those concerns for private credit and broader macroeconomic risks, MSDL is among the best positioned in the BDC space. Its solid balance sheet, very defensively positioned portfolio with strong underwriting performance, deep discount to NAV, along with buybacks, a well-covered and very attractive dividend yield, and well-tempered debt maturities, mean that I think the risk reward is exceptional for MSDL right now.

Given the very attractive risk-reward profiles for RYN and MSDL, they are among some of the highest conviction holdings in my core and retirement portfolios that yield between 7% and 8% and have below-market beta, along with impressive long-term total return outperformance at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Columbia Adaptive Risk Allocation Fund Q1 2026 Commentary
data.description:
The Morningstar Rating is for the indicated share classes only as of 03/31/26; other classes may have different performance characteristics. The Morningstar ratings for the overall, three-, five- and 10-year periods for Class A and Institutional Class Shares are 4 stars, 3 stars, 3 stars and 4 stars among 217, 217, 199 and 143 Tactical Allocation funds, and are based on a Morningstar Risk-Adjusted Return measure.

The relative strength in performance versus the benchmark was largely a result of exposure to inflation-hedged assets, like commodities and real estate-related securities (REITs).

Focuses on delivering more consistent returns

Allocates based on risk, not capital, to target enhanced diversification* and seek more consistent returns

Enhances diversification

Invests in a broad array of global asset classes to enhance diversification and potentially mitigate the effect of market volatility

Adaptive to market changes

Incorporates both tactical and dynamic flexibility to allow meaningful changes to risk exposures as market conditions change

*Diversification does not assure a profit or protect against loss. This is a specialized fund. Please see risk disclosure for important information.

We believe no single portfolio is appropriate for all market environments and have identified four distinct market states.

The market state classification spent the first two months of the year in bullish, then moved to neutral in March.

  • The first quarter of 2026 can be divided into two distinct phases. During the first two months, global equity markets extended their upward momentum. In contrast, March saw a sharp correction following the outbreak of the military conflict with Iran.
  • Global equity volatility increased, as the military conflict in the Middle East heightened uncertainty around risk premia across global markets.
  • Yield signals remained normal, but inflation-related developments warrant close monitoring for potential changes in trend.
    • Real yields remain positive, as nominal Treasury yields exceed headline inflation readings.
    • Our yield curve measure remains positive and exceeds threshold levels to qualify for a normal reading.

Fund performance

In the first quarter, Institutional Class shares (CRAZX) returned 2.46%, outperforming the Global 60/40 benchmark which generated a total return loss of 2.33%. On a currency hedged basis, the fund also outperformed its assigned Hedged Global 60/40 benchmark which returned -1.63%. The fund outperformed the assigned Morningstar tactical allocation category average, which returned 0.30% during the quarter.

  • The fund’s market state classification spent the first two months of the quarter in a bullish market state before moving into neutral for the final month of the quarter. A rise in global equity volatility helped move the market state into neutral for the month of March.
    • The market state selection contributed favorably to relative performance, versus a static neutral market state allocation, during the quarter.
  • The relative strength in performance versus the benchmark was largely a result of exposure to inflation-hedged assets, like commodities and real estate-related securities (REITs). This strength was offset somewhat by the relative weakness experienced across more traditional equity and core bond markets.
  • Consistent with policy-level positioning tied to the Neutral market state, the fund currently emphasizes a risk allocation that seeks more balance in terms of the contribution to risk coming from global equities, interest-rate-related fixed income, inflation-hedged assets and spread-related fixed income.
